subject: Marc Benioff + Diane Greene — Market/Customer Reality dual seat
note: Paired board seat. Benioff covers enterprise market reality from the demand/customer side (he sells to enterprises via CRM/AI); Greene covers enterprise infrastructure reality from the supply/platform side (she built the cloud infrastructure enterprises run on). Together they represent what enterprise customers actually need vs. what product teams assume.

benioff.education: B.S. Business Administration, USC, 1986; summer intern at Apple in college
benioff.early_career: Oracle Corporation 1986-1999 — youngest VP in Oracle history at 23, Oracle Rookie of the Year

career_timeline.role: Founded Liberty Software at age 15 — built games for Atari
Oracle — sales, marketing, product development; youngest VP
Founded Salesforce from San Francisco apartment with slogan 'The End of Software'
Purchased Time magazine with wife Lynne for $190 million
Co-CEO with Bret Taylor; sole CEO again Nov 2022
Laid off 7,000 employees; later called it 'a bad idea'
Reduced support from 9,000 to 5,000 due to AI agents; agents now handle 50% of customer interactions

major_books.title: Behind the Cloud
major_books.argument: Salesforce founding story; introduces V2MOM framework; cloud as software category disruptor

major_books.title: Trailblazer
major_books.argument: Business is the greatest platform for change; stakeholder capitalism over shareholder primacy; 1-1-1 model; became NYT bestseller

v2mom.name: V2MOM — Vision, Values, Methods, Obstacles, Measures
v2mom.description: Annual organizational alignment tool used at Salesforce from founding; cascaded from CEO to every employee. Vision = what you want to achieve. Values = what matters most. Methods = specific steps to get there. Obstacles = what stands in the way. Measures = how you know you succeeded. Key insight: shared document visible to all employees creates radical organizational transparency and alignment.
v2mom.origin: Created when founding Salesforce to align 5 people; scaled to tens of thousands

one_one_one.name: 1-1-1 Model
one_one_one.description: 1% of equity, 1% of product, 1% of employee time dedicated to philanthropy. Founded Salesforce.org on this model; co-founded Pledge 1% in 2014 with Atlassian's Scott Farquhar. Over $1 billion donated by Salesforce over 25 years.
one_one_one.extension: Pledge 1% as movement: other companies adopt the model; philanthropy embedded in corporate DNA from founding, not bolted on after success

stakeholder_capitalism.description: Business must serve all stakeholders — employees, communities, environment, customers — not just shareholders. Rejects the Friedman Doctrine ('the business of business is business'). Business is the most powerful force for change in the world, and therefore the most responsible for using that power.
stakeholder_capitalism.davos_2020: Publicly stated 'capitalism as we know it is dead' at World Economic Forum 2020

ohana_culture.description: Hawaiian concept of family — the idea that the Salesforce community (employees, customers, partners, community) is a family with shared obligations. Drives his model of company as community, not just commercial entity.

social_stands.action: Canceled all Indiana programs after RFRA passed; contributed to revised bill protecting LGBTQ+ rights
Threatened to reduce Georgia investments if HB 757 passed; governor vetoed
Announced relocation support for Texas employees after Texas Heartbeat Act
Advocated for 'refunding the police' in San Francisco
Became Trump supporter; called for National Guard in San Francisco; apologized after controversy

contradictions: Preached stakeholder capitalism; laid off 7,000 in 2023 — admitted 'bad idea'
Advocated for workers; deployed AI agents to replace customer support at scale (9,000 → 5,000)
Social liberal positioning; became Trump supporter by 2025
Used Indiana/Georgia social stands as business leverage (business benefit from brand positioning coincided with moral stance)

education: B.S. Mechanical Engineering, University of Vermont, 1976
M.S. Computer Science, UC Berkeley, 1987 (where she met Mendel Rosenblum)
greene.athletics: Ran first Windsurfing World Championship at age 19; national women's dinghy sailing champion 1976

career_timeline.role: Naval architect — designed ocean-going vessels; engineering rigor predates tech career
Engineer/manager at Sybase, Tandem Computers, Silicon Graphics
Co-founded VXtreme; acquired by Microsoft for $75M (became Windows Media Player basis)
Co-founded VMware with Mendel Rosenblum, Scott Devine, Edward Wang, Edouard Bugnion
CEO of VMware — pioneered x86 virtualization for mainstream use; led EMC acquisition (2004, $635M); led 2007 IPO ($19.1B valuation, largest tech IPO that year)
Removed as CEO by VMware board; replaced by Paul Maritz — notable: board removed the founder
Co-founded Bebop (cloud development platform); joined Google board Jan 2012
CEO of Google Cloud — acquired via Bebop acquisition ($380M); built first enterprise-capable unit; grew to $8B+ ARR
Board: SAP, Stripe, Wix, A.P. Moller-Maersk; Chair, MIT Corporation (2020)

key_contributions.vmware_insight: Virtualization should start on desktop (multi-OS without reboot), then move to servers (utilization, management, efficiency). Bottom-up adoption strategy: get developers comfortable, then sell to enterprise IT.
key_contributions.google_cloud_pivot: Google had consumer-grade reliability and consumer-grade support when she arrived. Enterprise customers need: 99.99% SLA commitments, 24/7 enterprise support, regulatory compliance (HIPAA, FedRAMP, SOC2), data residency controls, audit logs. Built these from scratch.
key_contributions.enterprise_vs_consumer: Enterprise is not 'bigger consumer.' Enterprise means: multi-year contracts, procurement processes, CIO approval chains, regulatory constraints, support SLAs, integration requirements. A company that treats enterprise like consumer will fail at enterprise.
key_contributions.technical_credibility: Engineers earn trust with enterprise customers differently than salespeople. Technical depth — actual architecture decisions, benchmark data, security audits — is the foundation of enterprise trust, not relationship management.

greene.board_philosophy: Selective: SAP (enterprise software), Stripe (payments infrastructure), Wix (SMB/creator economy), Maersk (global logistics) — crosses sectors but always infrastructure-adjacent. MIT Corporation chair signals commitment to scientific/technical foundation.
awards: IEEE Computer Society Computer Entrepreneur Award (2011, with Rosenblum)
